retrieved_docs.title: Management of Antithrombotics Around Gastrointestinal Procedures -- Clinical Significance -- Elective Endoscopy
retrieved_docs.content: In patients who need elective endoscopic procedures, if the indication for anti-thrombotic therapy is short-term, the procedure is best delayed until after that period. For patients on long-term or life-long antithrombotic treatment, the decision to temporarily hold the treatment for endoscopy should occur after a discussion with the patient and the concerned providers (primary medical provider, cardiologist, neurologist, or hematologist). In some high-risk patients, these agents cannot be interrupted, so the surgeon and patient must consider the risks and benefits of the procedure. See Figures. Anticoagulant Therapy and Antiplatelet Therapy Before Elective Endoscopic Procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Antithrombotics Around Gastrointestinal Procedures -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Antiplatelets and anticoagulants reduce the risk of venous thromboembolism in high-risk patients. However, when these patients need gastrointestinal procedures like endoscopy, these agents increase the risk of bleeding related to the procedure. This activity reviews an interprofessional approach to holding antithrombotic therapy before different gastrointestinal procedures and the resumption following the same.

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Antithrombotics Around Gastrointestinal Procedures -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the risk of bleeding associated with different gastrointestinal procedures. Evaluate the risks of thromboembolism in different medical conditions. Determine the approach to managing anticoagulants and antiplatelets before elective gastrointestinal procedures. Describe the interprofessional team approach to managing bleeding risks associated with antithrombotic agents in patients undergoing emergent endoscopy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Transurethral Resection of the Prostate -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Relative contraindications would be post-radiation therapy for prostate cancer, myasthenia gravis, multiple sclerosis, and Parkinson's, as these patient groups have a high likelihood of postoperative incontinence due to a dysfunctional external sphincter. Active anticoagulation is another relative contraindication to a traditional TURP, but a laser TURP can be performed on anticoagulated patients, or blood thinners can be appropriately withheld briefly to allow for the procedure. If a prostate is extremely large, greater than 100 grams, this is usually an indication to consider performing a simple prostatectomy or HoLEP, as most urologists do not possess the necessary skill or experience to perform a TURP of that size safely. A severely hyperactive bladder that is not easily controllable is also a relative contraindication.

retrieved_docs.title: Subacute Combined Degeneration of the Spinal Cord(Archived) -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Screening for vitamin B12 deficiency should be considered in patients receiving chronic treatment with proton pump inhibitors, H2 antagonists, and metformin. Patients with atrophic gastritis, gastrectomy, and pancreatectomy may also benefit from routine screening. In addition, preoperative screening for cobalamin deficiency should be considered for patients requiring anesthesia with nitrous oxide.

retrieved_docs.title: Enucleation -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: The surgeon should have a detailed discussion with the patient to explain the indications, risks, and benefits, as well as alternatives to enucleation. The patient will need to be aware of potential complications which are discussed in more detail under the complications section. If the patient is on nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agents, aspirin, or anticoagulants, then, if medically possible, these can be discontinued prior to the surgery. The patient's primary care provider will need to be informed regarding any adjustment of anticoagulation medication.

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Antithrombotics Around Gastrointestinal Procedures -- Clinical Significance -- Resumption Of Antithrombotic Therapy After Endoscopy
retrieved_docs.content: Anticoagulation should generally be resumed upon completion of the procedure unless there remains a continued risk of major bleeding. [1] [6] This concept is based on studies on warfarin and heparin products, with minimal literature regarding the resumption of DOACs. For patients on warfarin with a high risk of thromboembolism, the recommendation is for UFH or LMWH to be started at the earliest possible when there is no risk of major bleeding and continued until INR reaches a therapeutic level with warfarin. For patients at a lower risk of thromboembolism, warfarin should be restarted within 24 hours of the procedure. Because of the shorter duration of DOACs, if treatment with these agents cannot resume within 24 hours of a high-risk procedure, bridge therapy should be considered with UFH or LMWH in patients with a high risk of thrombosis. [21] In patients receiving DOACs for stroke prophylaxis in AF, the use of these agents can safely resume 1 day after low-risk procedures and 2 to 3 days after high-risk procedures without the need for bridging. [22] All antiplatelet agents should resume soon after hemostasis.

retrieved_docs.title: Rivaroxaban -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The major adverse effect of this drug is bleeding; hence, a thorough assessment of the patient is necessary before initiating this drug. The medical history of patients must rule out bleeding disorders, unstable blood pressure, chronic liver disease, chronic kidney disease, or anti-phospholipid antibody syndrome. Surgical history is necessary to rule out any heart valve replacements because such patients will benefit from taking warfarin. A drug history can rule out concomitant use of NSAIDs (nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs), clopidogrel, and other blood thinners because such patients have increased bleeding risk. Before starting this drug, the patient must receive screening for bleeding and coagulation profile and hepatic or renal impairment. A patient must receive counsel not to stop taking this drug without talking to the doctor as it may raise the chance of blood clots. Caution is necessary when performing any spinal or epidural procedure because such patients are more likely to have a bleed around the spine, and the patient must have monitoring for signs of nerve problems like pain, weakness, numbness or tingling, paralysis, or loss of bladder or bowel control after the procedure. The patient must understand that they may bleed easily while on this drug and need to avoid cuts and injury and to watch out for signs of bleeding like vomiting or coughing blood, bleeding from gums, dark stools or vaginal bleeding, headache or dizziness, and bleeding that takes a long time to stop.

retrieved_docs.title: Anticoagulation Safety -- Etiology -- Adverse Effects of Oral Anticoagulants
retrieved_docs.content: Patients on oral anticoagulants can experience subcutaneous, intramuscular, retroperitoneal, intracranial, gastrointestinal, and intraarticular bleeding. Patients receiving neuraxial anesthesia are at risk of developing a spinal epidural hematoma. Subclinical bleeding that would not typically cause a problem can present as clinically severe bleeding in a patient on anticoagulation. The risk of ICH increases with a history of stroke, hypertension, and cerebral amyloid angiopathy. Additional factors that increase the risk of bleeding include the following: Active peptic ulcer disease Thrombocytopenia with a platelet count lower than 50,000/µL Patients admitted to the intensive care unit Active cancer Rheumatologic disease Chronic kidney disease Liver disease An episode of bleeding in the last 3 months Presence of a central venous catheter Male sex Increasing age Concurrent antiplatelet medications Obesity Diabetes [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Singultus -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The duration of singultus, along with historical and physical findings, should guide additional imaging and diagnostic interventions. In patients with persistent or intractable singultus accompanied by neurologic symptoms or signs, brain imaging with computed tomography or magnetic resonance imaging may identify stroke, multiple sclerosis, tumor, syringomyelia, neuromyelitis optica, aneurysm, or vascular malformation. [46] In select cases, cerebrospinal fluid analysis may be required to evaluate for meningitis or encephalitis. Thoracic or abdominal computed tomography may reveal malignancy, aneurysm, abscess, or hernia. Referral for upper endoscopy is appropriate when singultus persists despite initial treatment with antacids or proton pump inhibitors (PPIs), particularly to assess for esophageal lesions such as malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Management of Antithrombotics Around Gastrointestinal Procedures -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Antithrombotic therapy, including antiplatelet and anticoagulants, is used to reduce the risk of thromboembolic episodes in patients with medical conditions like coronary artery disease, cerebrovascular accidents, peripheral arterial disease, atrial fibrillation, venous thromboembolism, hypercoagulable states, and mechanical heart valves among others. These agents increase the bleeding risk when undertaking endoscopic procedures, but there is also a risk of thromboembolic sequelae if they are withheld. The strategy for managing this therapy when these patients need gastrointestinal endoscopic procedures has been controversial. In this chapter, we discuss this based on the available evidence and the current guidelines. [1] [2] [3] The 2 vital factors to be considered while performing an endoscopic procedure in a patient on these agents are the risk of bleeding associated with the procedure and the risk of thromboembolism associated with the underlying medical condition for which the agents are being used.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 70 and Dextran 75 are plasma‑expanding agents that can help prevent venous thrombosis (التخثر الوريدي). However, both Dextran 70 and Dextran 75 are contraindicated in patients who have thrombotic disease (الاعتلال الخثري). This dual relationship underscores the importance of evaluating a patient’s underlying thrombotic condition before administering either Dextran 70 or Dextran 75, as their antithrombotic benefit in the venous system must be balanced against the risk of worsening an existing thrombotic disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2737
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 75, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease); using dextran 75 in this condition may promote clot formation and worsen clinical outcomes, so clinicians should avoid prescribing it to individuals who have thrombotic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2694
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 70, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 70 can increase blood viscosity and promote coagulation, its use in individuals who already have a propensity for clot formation may exacerbate the underlying thrombotic condition and increase the risk of serious vascular complications. Clinicians should therefore avoid administering Dextran 70 to patients diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this pro‑thrombotic risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2852
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 40, a low‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in the presence of الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 40 can increase blood viscosity and promote clot formation, its use in patients with thrombotic disorders may exacerbate the underlying condition and raise the risk of further vascular occlusion. Consequently, clinicians should avoid administering Dextran 40 to individuals diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1972
retrieved_docs.content: Cisaprid is contraindicated with both dextran 40 and dextran 75, meaning that these dextran formulations should not be co‑administered with cisaprid. Dextran 40 and dextran 75 each have antithrombotic activity and may prevent التخثر الوريدي (venous thrombosis). Because of this interaction, clinicians should avoid using cisaprid in patients who are receiving dextran 40 or dextran 75 for thrombosis prophylaxis, ensuring safe medication management and effective prevention of venous thrombotic events.
